tldr: Move from Backtrace's hosted crash management to Crashpad, the open-source minidump capture library used by Chromium. You trade a managed dashboard for full control over the capture pipeline plus a backend you provide.
why_switch: Eliminate per-seat or event-volume fees by self-hosting
Drop vendor lock-in on the capture client side
Standardize on the same crash plumbing used in Chrome
Open-source license keeps long-term roadmap predictable
Out-of-process handler survives many crashes that in-process tools miss
what_to_export: Existing minidump archives stored in Backtrace
Symbol files (.sym, PDB, dSYM) uploaded for symbolication
Project and application identifiers used by Backtrace SDKs
Custom attribute schemas attached to crash reports
Alert rules and Jira/Slack integration configuration
export_method: Use Backtrace's web UI or documented API to download crash reports, attached minidumps, and symbol files. If a bulk export endpoint is unavailable on your tier, contact Backtrace support to request a one-time data dump. Save symbols separately so you can feed them into your own symbolication pipeline later. For metadata (custom attributes, triage state), export per-project as CSV or JSON if the UI allows; otherwise script API pulls page-by-page. Keep raw minidumps untouched — they are portable across any Crashpad-compatible backend. Treat the export as archival only: Crashpad itself does not import historical reports, so plan to keep the Backtrace tenant read-only for a retention window while new crashes flow into the new system.
import_method: Crashpad is a client library, not a backend, so 'import' means rebuilding the receive side. Stand up a crash collector that accepts minidump uploads over HTTP multipart (the format Crashpad's handler posts). Options: an open-source receiver, a thin server you write, or pairing Crashpad with another product (Sentry, your own S3 + processing). Build Crashpad from the Chromium source tree following the project README, link the handler binary into your app's release bundle, and call CrashpadClient::StartHandler at startup with your collector URL and a local database path. Upload your symbol files into whatever symbolication tool you choose (e.g., minidump_stackwalk with a symbol store). Verify by triggering a synthetic crash in a staging build and confirming the minidump arrives and symbolicates.

concept_mapping.note: C++ API; wrappers needed for non-C++ stacks
Often a directory tree consumed by stackwalk tools

concept_mapping.target: Your own UI or paired backend

concept_mapping.note: Key/value pairs attached to the dump

concept_mapping.target: Built on top of your backend

gotchas: Crashpad has no dashboard — budget for a backend or a paired product before you cut over
Build system integration (gn/ninja or CMake forks) can be the longest single task on Windows
Symbolication is your responsibility; pipeline minidump_stackwalk or similar against your symbol store
Out-of-process handler must ship as a separate binary alongside the app; installer changes are required
Non-C++ apps need an FFI or wrapper; the official surface is C++
Historical Backtrace reports cannot be imported into Crashpad workflows — plan retention separately

email.subject: Notice of contract non-renewal — moving crash reporting in-house
email.body:
Hi team,

We are giving notice that we will not be renewing our Backtrace subscription at the end of the current term. Over the next 14 days we will complete migration to a self-hosted crash reporting pipeline built on Crashpad, which gives us direct control over capture, storage, and symbolication.

Before the cutover we would like to:

- Export all crash reports, minidumps, and symbol files from our tenant.
- Confirm the final invoice and any data retention obligations on your side.
- Keep the account in read-only mode for our agreed retention window so we can reference historical issues.

Please send the off-boarding checklist and confirm the contact for the data export. We appreciate the service over the past period and want to wrap up cleanly.

Thanks,
Your customer
